aiTitle: Discuss federation vs citizen model architecture

content.content:
1	# Org-Affiliated Credential Reorientation — Session Findings & Design Direction
2	
3	Date: 2026-05-22
4	
5	Status: **Exploration, not a decision.** Captured from a live design
6	conversation while first-running the ₣BM skirmish suite under a newly
7	org-affiliated Payor. Feeds ₢BSAAF (org-affiliated-account-reorientation).
8	Extends and reframes `memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md`.
9	
10	---
11	
12	## Why this memo exists
13	
14	The project's active Payor was moved from a consumer Google account
15	(`bhyslop@gmail.com`, no organization) to an org-affiliated Workspace identity
16	(`bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org`, organization `247899326218`). What looked like a
17	routine account switch surfaced a foundational truth: **the project's credential
18	model was built on a consumer-account assumption that organizations invalidate.**
19	This memo records what we proved, the reframe it forced, and the design
20	direction it points to — so the thinking can be resumed deliberately rather than
21	re-derived.
22	
23	---
24	
25	## 1. The foundational realization
26	
27	The RBRA pattern — mint a service-account key file, place it on disk, let
28	whatever holds it act as that role (Governor / Director / Retriever) — is the
29	bedrock the project stands on. It works beautifully for a solo developer on a
30	consumer account, and it required *zero* cloud-side identity machinery to
31	bootstrap. That was the right call to get started.
32	
33	But it is a **consumer-account** assumption. Serious organizations treat
34	downloadable, long-lived service-account keys as a security liability (they
35	never expire, they can leak, possession alone grants power). So secure-by-default
36	Workspace orgs **forbid creating them**. A project whose credential model
37	*requires* minting SA keys is therefore not merely "frictionful" in such orgs —
38	it is **structurally rejected** unless the operator deliberately weakens org
39	policy.
40	
41	Conclusion the conversation reached, plainly: **durable RBRA keyfiles are
42	anathema to serious orgs.** Keeping them as the *primary* mechanism is not a path
43	to acceptance in organizations the project does not itself administer. RBRA is
44	the consumer / bootstrap mode, not the org-native mode.
45	
46	---
47	
48	## 2. What an organization actually imposes (two independent policies)
49	
50	Empirically confirmed this session. These are **two separate dials**, not one
51	intertwined problem:
52	
53	| Policy | What it does | Where it bit us |
54	|--------|--------------|-----------------|
55	| **Cloud session / reauthentication control** | Periodically invalidates the session and forces the human to re-prove identity | Payor OAuth refresh token returned `invalid_rapt` after ~22h |
56	| **`iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation`** | Forbids creating downloadable SA keys | Governor mantle created the SA, granted it owner, then was refused the key |
57	
58	- **The reauth cadence is org-configurable**, not an OAuth law. A consumer
59	  account has no admin imposing it, which is why the gmail Payor's refresh token
60	  is effectively immortal and *has literally never needed reauthorization*. A
61	  Workspace admin can set the cadence anywhere from ~1h to "never expires."
62	- **The key-creation block is the structural one.** It is enforced by default on
63	  the org, inherited by every project under it.
64	
65	---
66	
67	## 3. Session evidence (the override experiment)
68	
69	We unblocked the standing depot by hand, to learn the shape of the problem:
70	
71	- Setting a **project-scoped override** clearing `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation`
72	  works — key creation succeeded ~60–90s after the policy write, and the full
73	  `canonical-invest` fixture then passed (governor mantle + retriever + director
74	  invests, real keys minted).
75	- But the override **requires `roles/orgpolicy.policyAdmin`, which is
76	  organization/folder-scoped only** — gcloud rejected a project-level grant
77	  outright (`Role ... is not supported for this resource`). So whoever performs
78	  the override on an org depot must wield **org-wide** Organization Policy
79	  Administrator authority.
80	- **This is a survival hack for an org you administer, not a strategy for orgs
81	  you do not.** The orgs the project wants to win over are precisely the ones
82	  that will not let you turn that policy off.
83	- Operational notes: the override is durable (persists until reverted); minted SA
84	  keys are long-lived (no reauth issue once created); the override is per-project
85	  (a fresh depot levy re-hits the wall); the mantle is **not idempotent** on the
86	  key-creation-failure path (it deletes and recreates the SA, grants owner, then
87	  fails — leaving a keyless-but-owner SA). The retriever/director invests are
88	  likewise not idempotent on rerun (HTTP 409 if the SA already exists). The
89	  idempotency gap is captured separately as a theurge fix (₢BMAAG).
90	
91	---
92	
93	## 4. The reframe: reauth is a compass, not an obstacle
94	
95	The central turn of the conversation. The reauth requirement is not noise the
96	org throws in the way — it is **the org telling you what it considers a
97	trustworthy credential**:
98	
99	> Trust must trace back to a human who recently proved they are present, and
100	> nothing durable should sit on disk.
101	
102	A downloaded SA key violates both halves (durable, and detached from any present
103	human). Reauth is the org continuously re-grounding the entire trust chain in a
104	live person. If you follow that compass instead of routing around it, you land
105	exactly where serious orgs want to be — and where central authorize/deauthorize
106	of roles becomes natural:
107	
108	- **No durable secret exists to steal.** The human holds a refresh token the org
109	  can age out; the roles hold nothing on disk.
110	- **Authorization is one IAM binding**, set centrally by an admin. Nothing to ship.
111	- **Deauthorization is one binding deletion** — instant, central, total. No key
112	  to hunt down, no rotation, no trusting old copies are gone.
113	- **Audit names the human** (`delegationInfo`: "Alice acted as director at
114	  14:03"), not the invisible-keyfile "the director did something."
115	
116	The thing that was annoying us all afternoon is, structurally, the doorway to
117	the model the project actually wants.
118	
119	---
120	
121	## 5. The target model
122	
123	**Human OAuth as the universal front door; roles stay service accounts, reached
124	by impersonation.** Critically, this is *not* "recast Director/Retriever to be
125	OAuth identities like Payor." The SA principals are preserved (their roles,
126	depot-scoped identity, audit trail, the RBSCIG/RBGI taxonomy all survive). What
127	changes is the *front door*: instead of holding a role's downloaded keyfile, a
128	human authenticates as themselves and asks Google to let them **act as** the role
129	SA for a short window (`generateAccessToken` on `iamcredentials.googleapis.com`).
130	
131	The load-bearing distinction — the one that prevents recreating the RBRA mess:
132	
133	- **The credential proves identity, not authority.** It says "you are Alice."
134	- **Authority lives centrally in IAM bindings**, granted/revoked in one place by
135	  an admin. The server decides, at action time, whether Alice may act in the role
136	  the action requires.
137	- If a credential's *contents* ever decide what the holder may do, RBRA has been
138	  rebuilt. If the credential only says *who* the holder is and the server
139	  consults central grants, it has not.
140	
141	This is also the architecture the 2026-04-27 memo proposed (Approach C). The
142	org-policy finding adds a trigger that memo never anticipated: it is no longer a
143	future *convenience*, it is the *only* path that survives on org accounts the
144	project does not administer.
145	
146	---
147	
148	## 6. Recasting RBRA (the vessel survives; the contents change)
149	
150	The credential file at a regime path survives as a concept. Its contents change,
151	and the natural template already exists: `RBRO` (the Payor) holds
152	`RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET` + `RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN`, not an SA key.
153	
154	The knot to untie: **you cannot mint a refresh token *per role*, because a
155	service account cannot do the browser dance — only a human can.** The browser
156	flow only ever produces *your* (human) refresh token. So the recast is *not*
157	"each RBRA holds its own long-lived secret." It collapses to:
158	
159	- **One human identity credential** (RBRO-shaped — the operator's refresh token),
160	  written by the authentication step.
161	- **Each role's RBRA becomes a secretless pointer**: "to act as Director,
162	  impersonate `director-…@…`." No key, no token — just the SA's address and the
163	  instruction to impersonate it.
164	- Authority to use the pointer is the IAM binding; revocation is its deletion.
165	
166	This is *better* than a one-for-one recast: it reduces the number of secrets to
167	one, rather than re-housing the same count in a new form.
168	
169	---
170	
171	## 7. The right axis: human-driven vs unattended (not org vs no-org)
172	
173	A tempting but wrong split is "no-org mode" vs "with-org mode." It is wrong
174	because **org-ness is not the project's to control** — it is a property of each
175	user's account, so branching on it means defending behavior the project cannot
176	predict.
177	
178	The defensible axis is the *workload*, which the project does control:
179	
180	- **Human-driven** (a person at a workstation, even a headless one): can always
181	  do the browser/device authentication and periodic reauth. Impersonation fits,
182	  on any org, **without needing to administer that org** — granting impersonation
183	  rights is an IAM binding the depot's own Governor controls, not an org-policy
184	  override requiring org-admin.
185	- **Unattended** (Cloud Build, CI with nobody present): cannot reauth, so it
186	  needs a *machine* identity (workload identity / build SA) — keyless, and
187	  org-sanctioned — regardless of org.
188	
189	Same two modes whether the user is on gmail or a Workspace never seen before.
190	Fewer permutations, keyed on something stable and owned.
191	
192	---
193	
194	## 8. The synthetic-human CI complication (the genuinely hard corner)
195	
196	The clean axis above has one exception specific to this project: **its
197	cross-platform CI must behave *as if a human* operates it** (Windows / macOS /
198	Linux), because the point is to validate the human-operator experience. A machine
199	identity would exercise a code path no real human walks. So this CI is
200	**unattended *and* must wear a human face** — a "synthetic human" the clean axis
201	does not allow for.
202	
203	Resolution, with honest costs:
204	
205	- It runs on an org **the project administers** (its own test org), so the
206	  session policy can be set long / never-expiring there, and the synthetic-human
207	  credential can be emplaced before a run and survive it untouched. **Contained
208	  to the project's own yard; never part of the shipped model.**
209	- "Very long lived" is a privilege grantable only on orgs you administer.
210	  End-users on strict orgs get *their* org's cadence, full stop.
211	- **Residual cost:** a long-lived human refresh token sitting on a CI box before
212	  a run is still a durable secret — a milder, revocable one, but durable. This is
213	  the one place the project does not fully escape "a long-lived secret on a
214	  machine." Acceptable for the test rig; not held up as the org-native ideal.
215	
216	Practical near-term mitigation (independent of any rework): the reauth window
217	only governs the *human Payor* OAuth; the long, slow parts of a run (cloud
218	builds via the Director SA, crucibles) use long-lived SA credentials and have no
219	reauth window. So a fresh authentication (or a cheap preflight probe that
220	attempts a token refresh) **right before** a run covers the human-touch prefix,
221	and the long tail rides credentials immune to the cadence.
222	
223	---
224	
225	## 9. Authentication mechanics: OOB is deprecated; Device flow is the successor
226	
227	- The "paste this URL in a browser, authenticate, paste the code back" step is the
228	  **OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant via the out-of-band (OOB) redirect**
229	  (`urn:ietf:wg:oauth:2.0:oob`). The pasted value is the *authorization code*.
230	  This is what `rbw-gPI` does today.
231	- **Google has deprecated OOB.** Building further on it is building on sand.
232	- The modern, non-deprecated, headless-friendly successor is the **OAuth 2.0
233	  Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628)** — the "go to this URL, enter this code"
234	  smart-TV flow. The browser need not be on the box; it need only exist somewhere
235	  the operator can reach.
236	- Crucially, the device flow **authenticates a human and produces a token tied to
237	  that human's identity** — the same identity model as OOB, just a better
238	  delivery channel. It introduces no new credential type and no authority-bearing
239	  artifact. Authority still lives in central IAM. It does **not** recreate the
240	  RBRA mess.
241	
242	---
243	
244	## 10. Acceptance in orgs the project does not control
245	
246	The acid test. In the target model:
247	
248	- A real human user on *any* org authenticates via the device flow, reauthing
249	  when their org's policy demands it (a human is present — fine).
250	- The admin of *their* org grants/revokes the person's roles centrally; the
251	  project ships no keys.
252	- The project never needs to weaken the user's org policy, because impersonation
253	  creates no SA keys — it sidesteps `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` entirely.
254	
255	This is why the reorientation matters for the project's success: it is the
256	difference between "works only where I am the org admin" and "works in any
257	serious org out of the box."
258	
259	---
260	
261	## 11. Open questions (to decide deliberately, feeding ₢BSAAF)
262	
263	- **Session-policy as a lever vs a constraint.** For the project's own test org,
264	  what should the Cloud session control be set to, and how is that documented as
265	  test-rig-only rather than model behavior? (Verify the current
266	  `scaleinvariant.org` setting — it lives in the Workspace Admin console.)
267	- **One human credential vs anything per-role.** Confirm the single-identity +
268	  impersonation-pointer shape, and what (if anything) the per-role RBRA file
269	  still holds once it carries no secret.
270	- **Does Payor stay distinct**, or does it become "a human granted the payor
271	  role" under the same model?
272	- **Coexistence & migration.** How RBRA-keyfile mode and impersonation mode
273	  coexist during transition (the 2026-04-27 memo's mode field), and whether
274	  keyfile mode is retained only for the consumer/bootstrap case.
275	- **The synthetic-human CI emplacement** mechanics, and where the residual
276	  long-lived-secret risk is documented and bounded.
277	- **Probe / handbook / regime surfaces** that assume keyfiles
278	  (RBSAJ/RBSAO/RBSAV, onboarding tracks, `rbw-gPR`'s stale "6-month idle" text).
279	- **The gauntlet coupling.** Any pace that levies a fresh depot (the full
280	  gauntlet) re-hits the key wall on the new project; skirmish (standing depot)
281	  does not. Regression during burn-down should lean on skirmish.
282	
283	---
284	
285	## 12. The one-line version
286	
287	The org didn't break a setting — it invalidated the assumption the credential
288	model was built on. RBRA keyfiles are the consumer/bootstrap mode. The org-native
289	mode is: a human proves identity (device flow), the project holds no role
290	secrets, roles are reached by impersonation, and authority is granted and revoked
291	centrally in IAM. Reauth is not the obstacle; it is the compass that forces the
292	model to be honest.
293	
294	---
295	
296	## 13. Resolutions (decided)
297	
298	Decided answers to §11, banked as they settle. Implementation heat TBD.
299	
300	### R1 — Payor stays distinct; the three lower tiers unify (resolves §11 "Does Payor stay distinct?")
301	
302	> **Superseded in part by R2:** R1's impersonation / `tokenCreator`-on-role-SA
303	> mechanism is replaced by direct human capability grants. R1's durable
304	> conclusions survive — Payor as ownership root, the erased credential axis, central
305	> revoke, org/no-org consistency; only "humans impersonate role SAs" is dropped.
306	
307	The keyless reorientation erases the *credential* axis that today's specs use to
308	set Payor apart (RBSGS: "the Payor stands apart"; OAuth vs RBRA keyfile). Once
309	Governor/Director/Retriever are reached by impersonation, all four tiers share the
310	human-OAuth front door — OAuth-vs-keyfile distinguishes nothing.
311	
312	What remains distinguishing Payor is non-credential and durable:
313	
314	- **Bootstrap root** — Payor's authority is project ownership, with no API path to
315	  bootstrap (RBSPE); the impersonation chain must bottom out on an authority that
316	  is not itself an impersonation grant. Payor is that floor.
317	- **Scope** — multi-depot + billing, vs Governor's single depot.
318	
319	Collapse statement: **Governor/Director/Retriever become one primitive — "a human
320	granted `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on a role SA by the tier above."
321	Payor is the axiom the recursion bottoms out on, authorized by ownership.**
322	
323	Verb redefinition implied:
324	
325	- **mantle** (Payor→Governor): project-owner-OAuth human grants `user:X`
326	  token-creator on the governor SA (was: create SA + mint downloadable key).
327	- **invest** (Governor→Dir/Ret): human impersonating the governor SA (which keeps
328	  owner-on-depot, RBSGM) grants `user:Y` token-creator on the dir/ret SA.
329	
330	Role SA principals persist (§5 "SA principals preserved"); only the human reach
331	changes from keyfile to impersonation.
332	
333	Org/no-org consistent: project ownership is the root in both worlds. Membership
334	eligibility differs only via `iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains` (an org may restrict
335	designees to its own domain).
336	
337	### R2 — Roles are direct human capability grants; SAs survive only as runtime identities (revises R1's mechanism)
338	
339	A role is a named set of IAM capabilities granted DIRECTLY to a human (`user:…`) on
340	the depot. Humans call APIs as themselves, carrying the capabilities the org
341	granted. No human ever impersonates a per-role service account.
342	
343	What survives from R1 unchanged: Payor is the ownership-rooted bootstrap floor; the
344	OAuth-vs-keyfile credential axis is erased (all humans use one browser/device
345	login); revocation is central (delete the human's grant); audit names the human —
346	now directly, not via `delegationInfo`; org/no-org consistent via project ownership
347	as the universal root.
348	
349	Verified separation that makes this sound (`rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:812`,
350	RBSDE-depot_levy):
351	
352	- Builds never ran as director. They run as **mason**, a per-depot runtime SA whose
353	  email is derived from depot config (`RBGD_MASON_EMAIL`). Director only submits
354	  builds and pushes the build-context pouch.
355	- Every director permission is `user:`-grantable — `cloudbuild.builds.editor`,
356	  `viewer`, `cloudbuild.workerPoolUser`, `serviceAccountUser` on mason (actAs),
357	  `artifactregistry.repoAdmin`. Retriever = `artifactregistry.reader` alone.
358	- Mason is impersonated only by Google's Cloud Build service agent
359	  (machine-to-machine), never by a human.
360	
361	Consequences:
362	
363	- Service accounts retreat to pure runtime plumbing (mason), addressed via
364	  depot-derived config, never held or worn by a human.
365	- Zero per-role files. The only on-disk human credential is the one OAuth login.
366	  The role-SA addressing/naming question — and the Governor timestamp — evaporates:
367	  there is no per-role SA a human names. This is the maximal form of §6 dissolution.
368	- Verb redefinition: mantle (Payor→Governor) = Payor grants a human the Governor
369	  capability-set (project `setIamPolicy`). invest (Governor→Dir/Ret) = Governor
370	  grants a human the Director/Retriever capability-set. Both are IAM grants naming
371	  `user:…`, no SA minting.
372	- Trade-off accepted: standing privilege rather than just-in-time elevation. Bounded
373	  by org reauth cadence; strictly better than today's durable keyfiles; JIT is
374	  addable later without re-architecture.
375	
376	Resolves §11 "one human credential vs anything per-role": one login, nothing
377	per-role at all.
378	
379	### R3 — Three credential models are tiers on one axis; MVP ships RBRA; the keyless successor is deferred and unchosen
380	
381	The option-map (not a committed ladder). Three ways an actor proves identity to
382	reach depot capabilities, along one axis:
383	
384	1. **RBRA keyfile** — consumer / bootstrap. Works only where SA-key creation is
385	   permitted (no-org, or an org that allows it). Already built. **MVP ships this.**
386	2. **Direct Google grants (R2)** — org baseline. Keyless. Users need *a*
387	   Google account (a free gmail suffices; they do not join the owner's org). Natural
388	   fit for Google-Workspace orgs, whose IdP already is Google.
389	3. **Federation** — org-only (federation pools are org-level). Users authenticate via
390	   an external OIDC/SAML IdP; no Google account; keyless. Opt-in / candidate paid
391	   tier. IdP is commoditized by *standards*, not vendor: GCP federation accepts any
392	   compliant OIDC/SAML issuer; self-hostable (Dex/Keycloak) or GCP Identity Platform
393	   as an in-house IdP. Abstraction boundary: "any OIDC/SAML issuer with stable
394	   subject claims."
395	
396	These are tiers, not alternatives. **The real cost is multi-mode coexistence**, not
397	any single tier — so the judicious frame is "which one keyless successor eventually
398	*replaces* RBRA," run one mode at a time, not an accreting stack.
399	
400	Decision: MVP ships RBRA (no rework). The keyless successor is **deferred and
401	unchosen** — do not commit build order while the complexity budget is tight. Bank
402	the map, choose the tier when org adoption actually demands keyless.
403	
404	Note: this federation is *ingress* identity (operators reaching the depot), distinct
405	from the roadmap's envoy entry, which uses federation for *egress* (GCP→AWS
406	cross-post). Same mechanism family, opposite direction — keep them unconflated.
407	
408	### R4 — Synthetic-human CI emplacement and test-org session policy (resolves §11 "synthetic-human CI emplacement" and "session-policy as a lever vs constraint")
409	
410	> **SUPERSEDED (2026-06-09) by `Memos/memo-20260609-federation-canon.md` D2.** The operator
411	> decided a human is always present to start runs, so the federation tier persists **no refresh
412	> token** — the caged test-rig residual below is **not built**. The tradeoff (no unattended
413	> scheduled federation runs) and its revisit-trigger live in the canon. R4's reasoning is retained
414	> as derivation of record.
415	
416	The two test-rig questions resolve together. A federated operator (Workforce
417	Identity Federation) authenticates by device flow; the resulting Google session
418	is capped at **12 hours** — a hard GCP limit, even on an org you own. So
419	month-scale unattended test machines are *not* a long session: they are a
420	**persisted IdP refresh token** that the machine silently re-exchanges every
421	≤12 h, headless. The human touch happens once, when the token is minted.
422	
423	Decision:
424	
425	- **Shipped human roles persist no refresh token** — device flow per session,
426	  reauth on the operator-org's own cadence. This is the org-native ideal.
427	- **The project's own test rig** (firewalled machines, project-administered test
428	  org) gets a **test-only enrollment** that persists one refresh token per
429	  machine — the single acknowledged durable-secret residual (§8). Set the test
430	  org's Workforce session to its 12 h maximum; the month-span comes from the
431	  refresh token, not the session.
432	- **Not security-breaking:** a federated/OAuth refresh token is not an SA key
433	  (org-legal, no policy override needed), is centrally revocable (disable the
434	  principal at the IdP — kills every machine at once), is capability-scoped, and
435	  is physically contained behind the firewall.
436	- This is a **federation-era** mechanism. Under MVP/keyfile the same durable
437	  test-credential need is met by the SA key itself (where key creation is
438	  permitted). Two eras, one need.
439	
440	Session policy is therefore a lever pulled only on the org you administer (the
441	test rig), documented as test-rig-only because end users always get *their* org's
442	cadence — never a project-set value.
443	
444	### R5 — Gauntlet coupling: regression leans on skirmish (resolves §11 "gauntlet coupling")
445	
446	Independent of credential tier. A full-gauntlet run levies a fresh depot, which
447	re-hits `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` on a secure-by-default org **even in
448	RBRA/keyfile mode**. Skirmish (standing depot) does not re-levy and does not hit
449	the wall.
450	
451	Decision: burn-down and routine regression **lean on skirmish**; full-gauntlet
452	fresh-depot levies run only on an account that permits SA-key creation (no-org,
453	or an org with the override). This is an operational testing posture, live for
454	MVP — not contingent on any keyless work.
455	
456	### §11 disposition (closure)
457	
458	All seven §11 questions are now decided or deliberately retired:
459	
460	| §11 question | Disposition |
461	|---|---|
462	| One human credential vs per-role | Resolved — R2 |
463	| Does Payor stay distinct | Resolved — R1 / R2 |
464	| Coexistence & migration | Resolved — R3 (mode-enum migration; see the credential-models memo) |
465	| Synthetic-human CI emplacement | Resolved — R4 |
466	| Session-policy lever vs constraint | Resolved — R4 |
467	| Gauntlet coupling | Resolved — R5 |
468	| Probe / handbook / regime keyfile surfaces (RBSAJ/RBSAO/RBSAV, onboarding, `rbw-gPR`) | **Retired** — R3 ships RBRA for MVP, so these surfaces stay correct as-is; no MVP rework. Re-decided if/when the keyless tier is chosen; the revisit-trigger lives in the RBSHR "Operator federation" roadmap entry. |
469	
470	The federation tier's mechanism — and the credential mode-enum and cult-verb
471	shape — are recorded separately in
472	`memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`.
473

file.content:
# Org-Affiliated Credential Reorientation — Session Findings & Design Direction

Date: 2026-05-22

Status: **Exploration, not a decision.** Captured from a live design
conversation while first-running the ₣BM skirmish suite under a newly
org-affiliated Payor. Feeds ₢BSAAF (org-affiliated-account-reorientation).
Extends and reframes `memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md`.

---

## Why this memo exists

The project's active Payor was moved from a consumer Google account
(`bhyslop@gmail.com`, no organization) to an org-affiliated Workspace identity
(`bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org`, organization `247899326218`). What looked like a
routine account switch surfaced a foundational truth: **the project's credential
model was built on a consumer-account assumption that organizations invalidate.**
This memo records what we proved, the reframe it forced, and the design
direction it points to — so the thinking can be resumed deliberately rather than
re-derived.

---

## 1. The foundational realization

The RBRA pattern — mint a service-account key file, place it on disk, let
whatever holds it act as that role (Governor / Director / Retriever) — is the
bedrock the project stands on. It works beautifully for a solo developer on a
consumer account, and it required *zero* cloud-side identity machinery to
bootstrap. That was the right call to get started.

But it is a **consumer-account** assumption. Serious organizations treat
downloadable, long-lived service-account keys as a security liability (they
never expire, they can leak, possession alone grants power). So secure-by-default
Workspace orgs **forbid creating them**. A project whose credential model
*requires* minting SA keys is therefore not merely "frictionful" in such orgs —
it is **structurally rejected** unless the operator deliberately weakens org
policy.

Conclusion the conversation reached, plainly: **durable RBRA keyfiles are
anathema to serious orgs.** Keeping them as the *primary* mechanism is not a path
to acceptance in organizations the project does not itself administer. RBRA is
the consumer / bootstrap mode, not the org-native mode.

---

## 2. What an organization actually imposes (two independent policies)

Empirically confirmed this session. These are **two separate dials**, not one
intertwined problem:

| Policy | What it does | Where it bit us |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Cloud session / reauthentication control** | Periodically invalidates the session and forces the human to re-prove identity | Payor OAuth refresh token returned `invalid_rapt` after ~22h |
| **`iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation`** | Forbids creating downloadable SA keys | Governor mantle created the SA, granted it owner, then was refused the key |

- **The reauth cadence is org-configurable**, not an OAuth law. A consumer
  account has no admin imposing it, which is why the gmail Payor's refresh token
  is effectively immortal and *has literally never needed reauthorization*. A
  Workspace admin can set the cadence anywhere from ~1h to "never expires."
- **The key-creation block is the structural one.** It is enforced by default on
  the org, inherited by every project under it.

---

## 3. Session evidence (the override experiment)

We unblocked the standing depot by hand, to learn the shape of the problem:

- Setting a **project-scoped override** clearing `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation`
  works — key creation succeeded ~60–90s after the policy write, and the full
  `canonical-invest` fixture then passed (governor mantle + retriever + director
  invests, real keys minted).
- But the override **requires `roles/orgpolicy.policyAdmin`, which is
  organization/folder-scoped only** — gcloud rejected a project-level grant
  outright (`Role ... is not supported for this resource`). So whoever performs
  the override on an org depot must wield **org-wide** Organization Policy
  Administrator authority.
- **This is a survival hack for an org you administer, not a strategy for orgs
  you do not.** The orgs the project wants to win over are precisely the ones
  that will not let you turn that policy off.
- Operational notes: the override is durable (persists until reverted); minted SA
  keys are long-lived (no reauth issue once created); the override is per-project
  (a fresh depot levy re-hits the wall); the mantle is **not idempotent** on the
  key-creation-failure path (it deletes and recreates the SA, grants owner, then
  fails — leaving a keyless-but-owner SA). The retriever/director invests are
  likewise not idempotent on rerun (HTTP 409 if the SA already exists). The
  idempotency gap is captured separately as a theurge fix (₢BMAAG).

---

## 4. The reframe: reauth is a compass, not an obstacle

The central turn of the conversation. The reauth requirement is not noise the
org throws in the way — it is **the org telling you what it considers a
trustworthy credential**:

> Trust must trace back to a human who recently proved they are present, and
> nothing durable should sit on disk.

A downloaded SA key violates both halves (durable, and detached from any present
human). Reauth is the org continuously re-grounding the entire trust chain in a
live person. If you follow that compass instead of routing around it, you land
exactly where serious orgs want to be — and where central authorize/deauthorize
of roles becomes natural:

- **No durable secret exists to steal.** The human holds a refresh token the org
  can age out; the roles hold nothing on disk.
- **Authorization is one IAM binding**, set centrally by an admin. Nothing to ship.
- **Deauthorization is one binding deletion** — instant, central, total. No key
  to hunt down, no rotation, no trusting old copies are gone.
- **Audit names the human** (`delegationInfo`: "Alice acted as director at
  14:03"), not the invisible-keyfile "the director did something."

The thing that was annoying us all afternoon is, structurally, the doorway to
the model the project actually wants.

---

## 5. The target model

**Human OAuth as the universal front door; roles stay service accounts, reached
by impersonation.** Critically, this is *not* "recast Director/Retriever to be
OAuth identities like Payor." The SA principals are preserved (their roles,
depot-scoped identity, audit trail, the RBSCIG/RBGI taxonomy all survive). What
changes is the *front door*: instead of holding a role's downloaded keyfile, a
human authenticates as themselves and asks Google to let them **act as** the role
SA for a short window (`generateAccessToken` on `iamcredentials.googleapis.com`).

The load-bearing distinction — the one that prevents recreating the RBRA mess:

- **The credential proves identity, not authority.** It says "you are Alice."
- **Authority lives centrally in IAM bindings**, granted/revoked in one place by
  an admin. The server decides, at action time, whether Alice may act in the role
  the action requires.
- If a credential's *contents* ever decide what the holder may do, RBRA has been
  rebuilt. If the credential only says *who* the holder is and the server
  consults central grants, it has not.

This is also the architecture the 2026-04-27 memo proposed (Approach C). The
org-policy finding adds a trigger that memo never anticipated: it is no longer a
future *convenience*, it is the *only* path that survives on org accounts the
project does not administer.

---

## 6. Recasting RBRA (the vessel survives; the contents change)

The credential file at a regime path survives as a concept. Its contents change,
and the natural template already exists: `RBRO` (the Payor) holds
`RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET` + `RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN`, not an SA key.

The knot to untie: **you cannot mint a refresh token *per role*, because a
service account cannot do the browser dance — only a human can.** The browser
flow only ever produces *your* (human) refresh token. So the recast is *not*
"each RBRA holds its own long-lived secret." It collapses to:

- **One human identity credential** (RBRO-shaped — the operator's refresh token),
  written by the authentication step.
- **Each role's RBRA becomes a secretless pointer**: "to act as Director,
  impersonate `director-…@…`." No key, no token — just the SA's address and the
  instruction to impersonate it.
- Authority to use the pointer is the IAM binding; revocation is its deletion.

This is *better* than a one-for-one recast: it reduces the number of secrets to
one, rather than re-housing the same count in a new form.

---

## 7. The right axis: human-driven vs unattended (not org vs no-org)

A tempting but wrong split is "no-org mode" vs "with-org mode." It is wrong
because **org-ness is not the project's to control** — it is a property of each
user's account, so branching on it means defending behavior the project cannot
predict.

The defensible axis is the *workload*, which the project does control:

- **Human-driven** (a person at a workstation, even a headless one): can always
  do the browser/device authentication and periodic reauth. Impersonation fits,
  on any org, **without needing to administer that org** — granting impersonation
  rights is an IAM binding the depot's own Governor controls, not an org-policy
  override requiring org-admin.
- **Unattended** (Cloud Build, CI with nobody present): cannot reauth, so it
  needs a *machine* identity (workload identity / build SA) — keyless, and
  org-sanctioned — regardless of org.

Same two modes whether the user is on gmail or a Workspace never seen before.
Fewer permutations, keyed on something stable and owned.

---

## 8. The synthetic-human CI complication (the genuinely hard corner)

The clean axis above has one exception specific to this project: **its
cross-platform CI must behave *as if a human* operates it** (Windows / macOS /
Linux), because the point is to validate the human-operator experience. A machine
identity would exercise a code path no real human walks. So this CI is
**unattended *and* must wear a human face** — a "synthetic human" the clean axis
does not allow for.

Resolution, with honest costs:

- It runs on an org **the project administers** (its own test org), so the
  session policy can be set long / never-expiring there, and the synthetic-human
  credential can be emplaced before a run and survive it untouched. **Contained
  to the project's own yard; never part of the shipped model.**
- "Very long lived" is a privilege grantable only on orgs you administer.
  End-users on strict orgs get *their* org's cadence, full stop.
- **Residual cost:** a long-lived human refresh token sitting on a CI box before
  a run is still a durable secret — a milder, revocable one, but durable. This is
  the one place the project does not fully escape "a long-lived secret on a
  machine." Acceptable for the test rig; not held up as the org-native ideal.

Practical near-term mitigation (independent of any rework): the reauth window
only governs the *human Payor* OAuth; the long, slow parts of a run (cloud
builds via the Director SA, crucibles) use long-lived SA credentials and have no
reauth window. So a fresh authentication (or a cheap preflight probe that
attempts a token refresh) **right before** a run covers the human-touch prefix,
and the long tail rides credentials immune to the cadence.

---

## 9. Authentication mechanics: OOB is deprecated; Device flow is the successor

- The "paste this URL in a browser, authenticate, paste the code back" step is the
  **OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant via the out-of-band (OOB) redirect**
  (`urn:ietf:wg:oauth:2.0:oob`). The pasted value is the *authorization code*.
  This is what `rbw-gPI` does today.
- **Google has deprecated OOB.** Building further on it is building on sand.
- The modern, non-deprecated, headless-friendly successor is the **OAuth 2.0
  Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628)** — the "go to this URL, enter this code"
  smart-TV flow. The browser need not be on the box; it need only exist somewhere
  the operator can reach.
- Crucially, the device flow **authenticates a human and produces a token tied to
  that human's identity** — the same identity model as OOB, just a better
  delivery channel. It introduces no new credential type and no authority-bearing
  artifact. Authority still lives in central IAM. It does **not** recreate the
  RBRA mess.

---

## 10. Acceptance in orgs the project does not control

The acid test. In the target model:

- A real human user on *any* org authenticates via the device flow, reauthing
  when their org's policy demands it (a human is present — fine).
- The admin of *their* org grants/revokes the person's roles centrally; the
  project ships no keys.
- The project never needs to weaken the user's org policy, because impersonation
  creates no SA keys — it sidesteps `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` entirely.

This is why the reorientation matters for the project's success: it is the
difference between "works only where I am the org admin" and "works in any
serious org out of the box."

---

## 11. Open questions (to decide deliberately, feeding ₢BSAAF)

- **Session-policy as a lever vs a constraint.** For the project's own test org,
  what should the Cloud session control be set to, and how is that documented as
  test-rig-only rather than model behavior? (Verify the current
  `scaleinvariant.org` setting — it lives in the Workspace Admin console.)
- **One human credential vs anything per-role.** Confirm the single-identity +
  impersonation-pointer shape, and what (if anything) the per-role RBRA file
  still holds once it carries no secret.
- **Does Payor stay distinct**, or does it become "a human granted the payor
  role" under the same model?
- **Coexistence & migration.** How RBRA-keyfile mode and impersonation mode
  coexist during transition (the 2026-04-27 memo's mode field), and whether
  keyfile mode is retained only for the consumer/bootstrap case.
- **The synthetic-human CI emplacement** mechanics, and where the residual
  long-lived-secret risk is documented and bounded.
- **Probe / handbook / regime surfaces** that assume keyfiles
  (RBSAJ/RBSAO/RBSAV, onboarding tracks, `rbw-gPR`'s stale "6-month idle" text).
- **The gauntlet coupling.** Any pace that levies a fresh depot (the full
  gauntlet) re-hits the key wall on the new project; skirmish (standing depot)
  does not. Regression during burn-down should lean on skirmish.

---

## 12. The one-line version

The org didn't break a setting — it invalidated the assumption the credential
model was built on. RBRA keyfiles are the consumer/bootstrap mode. The org-native
mode is: a human proves identity (device flow), the project holds no role
secrets, roles are reached by impersonation, and authority is granted and revoked
centrally in IAM. Reauth is not the obstacle; it is the compass that forces the
model to be honest.

---

## 13. Resolutions (decided)

Decided answers to §11, banked as they settle. Implementation heat TBD.

### R1 — Payor stays distinct; the three lower tiers unify (resolves §11 "Does Payor stay distinct?")

> **Superseded in part by R2:** R1's impersonation / `tokenCreator`-on-role-SA
> mechanism is replaced by direct human capability grants. R1's durable
> conclusions survive — Payor as ownership root, the erased credential axis, central
> revoke, org/no-org consistency; only "humans impersonate role SAs" is dropped.

The keyless reorientation erases the *credential* axis that today's specs use to
set Payor apart (RBSGS: "the Payor stands apart"; OAuth vs RBRA keyfile). Once
Governor/Director/Retriever are reached by impersonation, all four tiers share the
human-OAuth front door — OAuth-vs-keyfile distinguishes nothing.

What remains distinguishing Payor is non-credential and durable:

- **Bootstrap root** — Payor's authority is project ownership, with no API path to
  bootstrap (RBSPE); the impersonation chain must bottom out on an authority that
  is not itself an impersonation grant. Payor is that floor.
- **Scope** — multi-depot + billing, vs Governor's single depot.

Collapse statement: **Governor/Director/Retriever become one primitive — "a human
granted `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on a role SA by the tier above."
Payor is the axiom the recursion bottoms out on, authorized by ownership.**

Verb redefinition implied:

- **mantle** (Payor→Governor): project-owner-OAuth human grants `user:X`
  token-creator on the governor SA (was: create SA + mint downloadable key).
- **invest** (Governor→Dir/Ret): human impersonating the governor SA (which keeps
  owner-on-depot, RBSGM) grants `user:Y` token-creator on the dir/ret SA.

Role SA principals persist (§5 "SA principals preserved"); only the human reach
changes from keyfile to impersonation.

Org/no-org consistent: project ownership is the root in both worlds. Membership
eligibility differs only via `iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains` (an org may restrict
designees to its own domain).

### R2 — Roles are direct human capability grants; SAs survive only as runtime identities (revises R1's mechanism)

A role is a named set of IAM capabilities granted DIRECTLY to a human (`user:…`) on
the depot. Humans call APIs as themselves, carrying the capabilities the org
granted. No human ever impersonates a per-role service account.

What survives from R1 unchanged: Payor is the ownership-rooted bootstrap floor; the
OAuth-vs-keyfile credential axis is erased (all humans use one browser/device
login); revocation is central (delete the human's grant); audit names the human —
now directly, not via `delegationInfo`; org/no-org consistent via project ownership
as the universal root.

Verified separation that makes this sound (`rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:812`,
RBSDE-depot_levy):

- Builds never ran as director. They run as **mason**, a per-depot runtime SA whose
  email is derived from depot config (`RBGD_MASON_EMAIL`). Director only submits
  builds and pushes the build-context pouch.
- Every director permission is `user:`-grantable — `cloudbuild.builds.editor`,
  `viewer`, `cloudbuild.workerPoolUser`, `serviceAccountUser` on mason (actAs),
  `artifactregistry.repoAdmin`. Retriever = `artifactregistry.reader` alone.
- Mason is impersonated only by Google's Cloud Build service agent
  (machine-to-machine), never by a human.

Consequences:

- Service accounts retreat to pure runtime plumbing (mason), addressed via
  depot-derived config, never held or worn by a human.
- Zero per-role files. The only on-disk human credential is the one OAuth login.
  The role-SA addressing/naming question — and the Governor timestamp — evaporates:
  there is no per-role SA a human names. This is the maximal form of §6 dissolution.
- Verb redefinition: mantle (Payor→Governor) = Payor grants a human the Governor
  capability-set (project `setIamPolicy`). invest (Governor→Dir/Ret) = Governor
  grants a human the Director/Retriever capability-set. Both are IAM grants naming
  `user:…`, no SA minting.
- Trade-off accepted: standing privilege rather than just-in-time elevation. Bounded
  by org reauth cadence; strictly better than today's durable keyfiles; JIT is
  addable later without re-architecture.

Resolves §11 "one human credential vs anything per-role": one login, nothing
per-role at all.

### R3 — Three credential models are tiers on one axis; MVP ships RBRA; the keyless successor is deferred and unchosen

The option-map (not a committed ladder). Three ways an actor proves identity to
reach depot capabilities, along one axis:

1. **RBRA keyfile** — consumer / bootstrap. Works only where SA-key creation is
   permitted (no-org, or an org that allows it). Already built. **MVP ships this.**
2. **Direct Google grants (R2)** — org baseline. Keyless. Users need *a*
   Google account (a free gmail suffices; they do not join the owner's org). Natural
   fit for Google-Workspace orgs, whose IdP already is Google.
3. **Federation** — org-only (federation pools are org-level). Users authenticate via
   an external OIDC/SAML IdP; no Google account; keyless. Opt-in / candidate paid
   tier. IdP is commoditized by *standards*, not vendor: GCP federation accepts any
   compliant OIDC/SAML issuer; self-hostable (Dex/Keycloak) or GCP Identity Platform
   as an in-house IdP. Abstraction boundary: "any OIDC/SAML issuer with stable
   subject claims."

These are tiers, not alternatives. **The real cost is multi-mode coexistence**, not
any single tier — so the judicious frame is "which one keyless successor eventually
*replaces* RBRA," run one mode at a time, not an accreting stack.

Decision: MVP ships RBRA (no rework). The keyless successor is **deferred and
unchosen** — do not commit build order while the complexity budget is tight. Bank
the map, choose the tier when org adoption actually demands keyless.

Note: this federation is *ingress* identity (operators reaching the depot), distinct
from the roadmap's envoy entry, which uses federation for *egress* (GCP→AWS
cross-post). Same mechanism family, opposite direction — keep them unconflated.

### R4 — Synthetic-human CI emplacement and test-org session policy (resolves §11 "synthetic-human CI emplacement" and "session-policy as a lever vs constraint")

> **SUPERSEDED (2026-06-09) by `Memos/memo-20260609-federation-canon.md` D2.** The operator
> decided a human is always present to start runs, so the federation tier persists **no refresh
> token** — the caged test-rig residual below is **not built**. The tradeoff (no unattended
> scheduled federation runs) and its revisit-trigger live in the canon. R4's reasoning is retained
> as derivation of record.

The two test-rig questions resolve together. A federated operator (Workforce
Identity Federation) authenticates by device flow; the resulting Google session
is capped at **12 hours** — a hard GCP limit, even on an org you own. So
month-scale unattended test machines are *not* a long session: they are a
**persisted IdP refresh token** that the machine silently re-exchanges every
≤12 h, headless. The human touch happens once, when the token is minted.

Decision:

- **Shipped human roles persist no refresh token** — device flow per session,
  reauth on the operator-org's own cadence. This is the org-native ideal.
- **The project's own test rig** (firewalled machines, project-administered test
  org) gets a **test-only enrollment** that persists one refresh token per
  machine — the single acknowledged durable-secret residual (§8). Set the test
  org's Workforce session to its 12 h maximum; the month-span comes from the
  refresh token, not the session.
- **Not security-breaking:** a federated/OAuth refresh token is not an SA key
  (org-legal, no policy override needed), is centrally revocable (disable the
  principal at the IdP — kills every machine at once), is capability-scoped, and
  is physically contained behind the firewall.
- This is a **federation-era** mechanism. Under MVP/keyfile the same durable
  test-credential need is met by the SA key itself (where key creation is
  permitted). Two eras, one need.

Session policy is therefore a lever pulled only on the org you administer (the
test rig), documented as test-rig-only because end users always get *their* org's
cadence — never a project-set value.

### R5 — Gauntlet coupling: regression leans on skirmish (resolves §11 "gauntlet coupling")

Independent of credential tier. A full-gauntlet run levies a fresh depot, which
re-hits `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` on a secure-by-default org **even in
RBRA/keyfile mode**. Skirmish (standing depot) does not re-levy and does not hit
the wall.

Decision: burn-down and routine regression **lean on skirmish**; full-gauntlet
fresh-depot levies run only on an account that permits SA-key creation (no-org,
or an org with the override). This is an operational testing posture, live for
MVP — not contingent on any keyless work.

### §11 disposition (closure)

All seven §11 questions are now decided or deliberately retired:

| §11 question | Disposition |
|---|---|
| One human credential vs per-role | Resolved — R2 |
| Does Payor stay distinct | Resolved — R1 / R2 |
| Coexistence & migration | Resolved — R3 (mode-enum migration; see the credential-models memo) |
| Synthetic-human CI emplacement | Resolved — R4 |
| Session-policy lever vs constraint | Resolved — R4 |
| Gauntlet coupling | Resolved — R5 |
| Probe / handbook / regime keyfile surfaces (RBSAJ/RBSAO/RBSAV, onboarding, `rbw-gPR`) | **Retired** — R3 ships RBRA for MVP, so these surfaces stay correct as-is; no MVP rework. Re-decided if/when the keyless tier is chosen; the revisit-trigger lives in the RBSHR "Operator federation" roadmap entry. |

The federation tier's mechanism — and the credential mode-enum and cult-verb
shape — are recorded separately in
`memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`.

content.text: Now reading the remaining lineage memo — the 260522 reorientation that holds the original derivation (R1–R5) of why the keyfile tier exists at all.

Workforce Identity Federation is available as a no-cost feature. This means there is no per-user, per-month cost for the core Workforce Identity Federation service itself.

aiTitle: Discuss federation vs citizen model architecture

content.text:
Research done — and one of the facts I verified materially reframes your question. Here's where I land.

## The short version

You're right that the domain is a small ask — but the domain was never federation's real per-user obligation. **Workforce federation requires every user to bring an external IdP**, and your MVP persona (the solo evaluator) doesn't have one. The misgiving about the citizen tier is legitimate; the place it naturally leads, though, may not be workforce federation but the **third model your own 260522 memo already mapped (R2: direct grants to Google identities)** — which the citizen-tier decision quietly buried.

## What I verified

- Workforce Identity Federation is [no-cost](https://cloud.google.com/workforce-identity-federation), and the org it requires is free via [Cloud Identity Free](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/how-to/set-up-cloud-identity-admin) (domain + [TXT-record verification](https://support.google.com/cloudidentity/answer/7331243?hl=en), 50 free users). So yes: namecheap, ~$10/yr, an hour of ceremony. That part of your reframe holds.
- But the [workforce federation docs](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation) are consistent: pools trust an **external** OIDC/SAML IdP (Entra, Okta, Keycloak…). Google's own identities don't ride workforce pools — the feature exists precisely to federate *non-Google* directories. A solo evaluator would have to stand up an IdP tenancy ([free Entra tenant](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity-platform/v2-oauth2-device-code), Auth0 free tier, self-hosted Keycloak) — a *second* identity service, with its own signup, app registration, and lifecycle — to federate an org of one into a depot of one. You'd be trading "manage a credential model" for "operate an identity provider," and for the evaluator that's fake bureaucracy.
- The reason the canon's design needs that external IdP at all: Google's own [device flow](https://developers.google.com/identity/protocols/oauth2/limited-input-device) supports only `openid`/`email`/`profile`/Drive/YouTube scopes — **no cloud-platform scope** — and OOB is dead. So a native Google identity can't do the headless "click on your phone" login in your curl-only stack; an external IdP's device flow plus STS exchange restores it. Federation's IdP requirement is partly a workaround for that Google limitation.

## What going federation-only would actually buy you

Your misgiving has real teeth, and it's worth saying plainly what dies with the citizen tier:

- The entire keyfile estate: SA minting, key-last enfranchise, recut, verb-internal delivery, RBRA full-auto authorship, the key-at-rest blast radius and its propagation-lag caveats.
- The two-tiers-forever maintenance: the RBRD mode enum, dual accessor branches, dual test matrices, dual onboarding tracks. D1's "both ship forever" is the single most expensive cinch in the canon.
- The security wart itself: D6's contrast becomes "the product holds zero durable secrets, period" — a clean story for a security-focused product.
- And per the 260522 memo, serious orgs **structurally reject** keyfiles anyway (`disableServiceAccountKeyCreation`), so the citizen tier's constituency was only ever the solo/consumer user — the same person the federation ceremony punishes most.

That last point is the crux: keyfile's only customer is the evaluator, and federation's worst victim is the evaluator. Neither tier serves your funnel's first hour well.

## The middle this re-opens

If you're willing to require the org (your reframe), the simplest secret-free model isn't federation — it's the 260522 memo's **R2: direct IAM grants to Google identities** (`user:alice@her-domain.com`). No SA keys, no recut, no workforce pool, no attribute mapping, no STS, no external IdP. Enfranchise becomes an IAM binding; expel becomes its deletion; the terrier, enfeoff/escheat, capability-sets — the whole civic layer — survive untouched, because they were built tier-blind. Revocation is central. Audit names the human.

Its one genuine blocker is **login mechanics**: with device flow scope-barred, a native-identity session in your gcloud-free stack needs the loopback browser flow (browser on the same box — breaks your SSH'd test machines) or an in-memory-only refresh token, or an SSH port-forward dance. That's an engineering question with a knowable answer, not an architecture question — and it's spike-sized.

The honest comparative frame, all three org-era options:

| | Keyfile (current ₣BZ) | Native grants (R2) | Workforce federation |
|---|---|---|---|
| User prerequisite | nothing | domain + Cloud Identity | domain + org + **external IdP** |
| Durable secret | one key per citizen | none | none |
| Headless login | n/a (key at rest) | **hard** (no device flow for cloud scopes) | clean (IdP device flow) |
| Serious-org compatible | **no** (key policy) | yes | yes |
| Enterprise bring-your-own-IdP | no | no (users need Google identities) | **yes** |

Federation stays the right *enterprise* tier — that's where the IdP already exists and identity-duplication is the thing being avoided. The question your misgiving actually poses is whether the *other* tier should be keyfile (no-org) or native-grants (org-required).

## What this does and doesn't disturb

The paddock was built for exactly this wobble: movements 1–2 (accessor seam, capability-sets) are cinched as no-regret under every model variant, and the civic layer is tier-blind by construction. The decision only has to land before movement 3 is slated — that's where enfranchise/recut/key-delivery commit to keyfile mechanics. So nothing currently in flight is wasted, and there's no urgency tax on deciding carefully.

My read, sincerely held but lightly: dropping the citizen tier is more defensible than the canon's D1 made it look, because its only constituency is also the constituency the org ceremony hurts least *in absolute terms* ($10 + an hour, once) — but the replacement should probably be R2 native grants, not federation-for-everyone, and the load-bearing unknown is the headless login story. A spike that walks the full evaluator ceremony end-to-end (namecheap → Cloud Identity → first authenticated depot call, timed) plus a loopback-flow-over-SSH experiment would turn this from judgment into evidence. The test-rig consequence deserves eyes too: every keyless variant makes theurge's personas need a live human session, which is consistent with the D2 premise but presses on the canon's unattended-runs revisit-trigger immediately.

Want me to capture this as a paddock note on ₣BZ (the open question + spike shape), or keep discussing first?

Sources: [Workforce Identity Federation overview](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation) · [Workforce Identity Federation product page (no-cost)](https://cloud.google.com/workforce-identity-federation) · [Google device flow scope limits](https://developers.google.com/identity/protocols/oauth2/limited-input-device) · [Cloud Identity setup](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/how-to/set-up-cloud-identity-admin) · [Domain verification](https://support.google.com/cloudidentity/answer/7331243?hl=en) · [Entra device code flow](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity-platform/v2-oauth2-device-code)

I don't want to consider anything other than full IdP (though we might only test with Google's, given the full standardization of this protocol) and _maybe_ a baby step to get there closer to current RBRA.  Recast given this strong premise.  You can argue it if you are inclined, though.

content.text: Good — that's a firm premise to recast against, and it actually makes the pivot *smaller* than my last message implied, because the paddock pre-fenced exactly this. Two facts to pin down first, since your "test with Google's IdP" idea hinges on them: whether workforce pools can take an uploaded JWKS / private issuer (Keycloak-in-a-crucible question) and whether Identity Platform can serve as the workforce IdP (the in-house-Google test path).

JSON Web Key Set and JWKS URI Explained","url":"https://www.authgear.com/post/what-is-jwks/"},{"title":"JSON Web Key Sets (JWKS) - Azure AD Workload Identity","url":"https://azure.github.io/azure-workload-identity/docs/installation/self-managed-clusters/oidc-issuer/jwks.html"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about workforce identity pool OIDC providers and JWKS configuration:

## Key Findings

**JWKS Upload and Issuer URI:**

You can update OIDC JWKs using the `gcloud iam workforce-pools providers update-oidc` command with `--jwk-json-path` parameter, where you replace JWK_JSON_PATH with the path to the JWKs JSON file.

JWK_JSON_PATH is an optional path to a locally uploaded OIDC JWKs. If this parameter isn't supplied, Google Cloud instead uses your IdP's /.well-known/openid-configuration path to source the JWKs containing the public keys.

**Issuer URI Requirements:**

The OIDC issuer URI must be in a valid URI format and start with https; for example, https://example.com/oidc.

**Publicly Reachable Requirements:**

If Google Cloud is unable to fetch your IdP's OIDC metadata document or JWKS, this error usually occurs because the endpoints aren't configured to be reachable from the public internet. To resolve this error, check that the OIDC endpoint is publicly available and compliant with the OIDC specification.

**Limitations of Uploaded JWKS:**

Local OIDC JWKs can be uploaded through implicit flow or code flow, but can only be used in programmatic flow. You can't use local OIDC JWKs when signing in to the console (federated).

I've completed the web search for your query about Google Identity Platform as a workforce identity federation IdP. Here are the key findings:

## Workforce Identity Federation Overview

Workforce Identity Federation is an IAM feature that lets you configure and secure granular access for your workforce—employees and partners—by federating identities from an external identity provider (IdP).

## Identity Platform vs Workforce Identity Federation

Identity Platform is intended for building enterprise-focused SaaS applications. It lets you integrate with enterprise IdPs using inbound OIDC or SAML.

## Supported IdP Protocols

Workforce Identity Federation can be used with any IdP that supports OpenID Connect (OIDC) or SAML 2.0, such as Microsoft Entra ID, Active Directory Federation Services (AD FS), Okta, and others.

## Authentication Flow

The magic of Workforce Identity Federation lies in a secure token exchange process, facilitated by GCP's Security Token Service (STS).

## Key Capabilities

Workforce Identity Federation can enable your organization's users to access Google Cloud through the same login experience they already use for their existing IdP for single sign-on. Workforce Identity Federation also can enable fine-grained access through attribute mapping and attribute conditions.

Note: My search results don't contain specific information about `securetoken.google.com` as an issuer or the REST API endpoints like `signInWithPassword`. If you need specific technical details about those components, please let me know and I can perform additional searches.

content.text:
Both facts landed, and one is genuinely good news for your test story: workforce providers accept an **uploaded JWKS** (`--jwk-json-path`), which works for the programmatic flow even when the issuer isn't publicly reachable — console sign-in is what it can't do, and RBK doesn't need console. That makes a hermetic, container-homed test IdP viable. Here's the recast.

## Recast under the premise: federation is the form, the only question is the bridge

**The pivot is smaller than it looks, because the paddock pre-fenced it: movements 1–2 *are* the baby step, and the verb-robustness table's two contingency columns — "Federation arrives" and "RBRA dropped entirely" — simply both fire.** With keyfile never shipping as a tier, D1 dies (one tier, org-required), and the RBRD mode enum dies with it — no tamper-evident downgrade surface, no dual test matrix, no two-tiers-forever.

What changes, concretely:

- **Movements 1–2 unchanged, verbatim.** The accessor seam refactors over today's keyfiles with zero behavior change; capability-sets transcribe out of the cult verbs. They were cinched as no-regret under every variant — this is the variant. They are also the only "step closer to current RBRA" that earns its existence: they make today's machinery *wear the final interface* without adding any keyfile flesh.
- **Movement 3 sheds its keyfile body and gets a federation body.** Sheds: recut (the IdP owns rotation — the paddock's table already says "retires"), verb-internal key delivery and the whole assay-retirement mechanics, key-last enfranchise ordering, RBRA full-auto authorship discipline. The civic verbs get *simpler*: enfranchise = IAM grant on the `principal://` subject + terrier write; expel = escheat-all + deregister. Gains: the payor **federation-setup verb** (pool/provider/attribute-mapping, homed in `rbw-m` at levy), device-flow Leg 1 + STS Leg 2 inside the accessor, the session cache + headless fail-fast membrane, RBRD federation facts.
- **Everything tier-blind survives untouched**: terrier + Terrier bucket, enfeoff/escheat on named capability-sets, `rbw-p`/`rbw-m` families, audit-as-mirror, RBS0 civic quoins. The identity-kind word shifts citizen→federate; the civic structure doesn't notice.
- **Bridge policy**: the existing keyfile estate is *untouched legacy* riding under the movement-1 accessor — suites stay green on keys while federation is built beside them. Cutover happens when federation personas pass the same suites; then the RBRA estate retires **whole** (cult verbs, key machinery, RBSRA) as its own movement. Keyfile is never maintained as a tier, never shipped as a citizen model — it's scaffolding with a demolition date, which is exactly what the Palisade discipline asks of a workaround.

## On "test only with Google's IdP" — I'll push back here

Google doesn't really have a first-party IdP for this: `accounts.google.com` is excluded from workforce pools by design, and Identity Platform as a workforce IdP is **unverified** — docs position it for SaaS apps; nothing confirms the pairing (spike-sized question, but don't lean on it). The standardization argument cuts the other way: since the protocol is the commodity, test against a *conformant IdP you control* — and the JWKS-upload finding makes **Keycloak in a crucible** the natural lab rat. It's hermetic, free, fully RFC 8628-capable, and thematically it's your own dogfood — the test IdP is itself a bottle. A free Entra tenant as a one-time manual conformance cross-check covers the "real foreign IdP" angle.

## The bite to name honestly

The synthetic-human problem returns the moment keys retire: theurge's personas need sessions. Options are a human click per run (your D2 premise, sessions configurable to 12h on your own test org — likely fine for how you actually run suites) or a test-rig-only durable secret (Keycloak password grant against your own container — R4's ghost in milder clothes). That's a decision to make deliberately at movement-3 slating, not a blocker. And the evaluator-onboarding recipe (domain + Cloud Identity + an IdP tenancy) becomes a docs duty — an opinionated free-tenant walkthrough — but that's a movement-5 question.

## Arguing it, since you offered

Only mildly. The keyfile stepping stone was buying two things: suites that stay green with zero ceremony (the bridge policy preserves this), and an MVP shippable independent of federation unknowns (this you're consciously forgoing). The unknowns are Palisade-grade — pool/provider REST behavior, STS quirks, IdP variance, the Identity Platform question — so the one structural ask I'd make: a **spike pace before movement 3 is slated** that stands up pool + provider + a test IdP on your existing org and walks Leg 1/Leg 2 in pure curl, end to end. If that spike lands, the straight shot is strictly better than building recut-and-delivery machinery whose only customer would have been the bridge. I started yesterday's conversation skeptical on the evaluator-funnel cost; under your premise that the funnel *should* select for operators willing to hold a domain and an IdP, the citizen keyfile tier loses its last constituency — I have no remaining argument for building it.

Want me to bank this as a paddock revision on ₣BZ — the premise shift (D1 revised to single-tier org-required), the movement-3 recast, the bridge/demolition policy, the test-IdP election, and the spike as a new open? You'd review the wording before I write it.

Sources: [Manage workforce pool providers (JWKS upload, programmatic-flow-only limitation)](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/manage-workforce-identity-pools-providers) · [Workforce Identity Federation overview](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation) · [Identity products comparison (Identity Platform positioning)](https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/identity-products) · [Obtaining short-lived workforce tokens](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials)

## Free Tier / Free Plan Comparison

**Auth0 (Okta Customer Identity Cloud):**
The free tier supports up to 25,000 MAUs with core features, though another source mentions a free tier limited to 7,000 MAU with basic features. Auth0 counts a user as "active" if they log in at least once in a calendar month, and you pay for that user.

**Okta Workforce Identity:**
Okta uses a per-user subscription model with annual contracts and a $1,500 minimum, with pricing starting at $6 per user per month for the Starter plan. Okta does not appear to offer a free tier for workforce identity.

**Microsoft Entra ID:**
Entra ID Free is included with every Microsoft 365 subscription, and Entra ID P1 costs $6/user/month and P2 costs $9/user/month.

content.text:
Short answers, in order — then go to bed.

**Which first: Microsoft Entra ID, with a free tenant.** It's the most popular enterprise workforce IdP by a wide margin (Entra and Okta dominate the segment; Entra ships with every Microsoft 365/Azure footprint), which means it's also the IdP your real federation-tier customers most likely already run — testing against it first validates the path most adopters will actually walk. A standalone Entra tenant is free, no Azure spend required, full RFC 8628 device-flow support, and Google maintains a [dedicated Entra + workforce federation setup guide](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-sign-in-microsoft-entra-id) — you'd be on the most-trodden integration path Google documents. Okta proper has no free tier ([$1,500/yr minimum](https://www.okta.com/pricing/)); Auth0's free tier is generous but it's a customer-identity product, less representative of your federation customer. Keycloak-in-a-crucible stays the hermetic CI rat alongside — Entra is the "real foreign IdP" leg, Keycloak the repeatable one.

**Would swapping IdPs "just work" via an RBRD twiddle?** The *code* will be IdP-agnostic by design — and genuinely so, because the surface RBK touches is small and standardized: OIDC discovery (`.well-known/openid-configuration` hands you the device-authorization and token endpoints), RFC 8628 polling, an ID-token JWT out, and the STS exchange is IdP-blind entirely. RBRD holds issuer + client ID + pool/provider facts; pointing at a different conformant IdP needs no code change. But I want to set the expectation precisely: **switching IdPs is an administrative re-federation, not a config twiddle.** Two reasons:

1. **Trust is pool-side, not station-side.** A new IdP means the payor runs federation-setup again — new provider in the pool, new attribute mapping. That's a payor ceremony, correctly so: the thing changing *is* the depot's root of trust.
2. **Grants are bound to subjects.** Every IAM binding names `principal://...pool/subject/X`, and the subject comes from the IdP's immutable claim (`oid` on Entra, `sub` elsewhere). A new IdP mints new subjects, so every citizen gets re-enfranchised. The terrier makes this mechanical — it's exactly the "re-run idempotent enfeoff" heal — but it's a migration event, not a reboot.

And one Palisade note: real IdPs have small quirks at the edges (optional `verification_uri_complete`, `slow_down` polling behavior, which scopes they demand, public-client token-endpoint auth). Conformant ones converge well, but plan for a thin per-IdP quirk membrane rather than promising bug-for-bug uniformity — characterize, contain, log, the usual conduct.

So the honest pitch to your users: *any conformant OIDC IdP works; choosing one is a depot-founding decision; changing it later is supported and mechanical but is a governor/payor ceremony, not a knob.* That's the right shape anyway — you don't want root-of-trust changes to feel like config twiddles.

Everything from tonight (premise shift, movement-3 recast, bridge/demolition policy, Entra-first + Keycloak-crucible test election, the pre-movement-3 spike) is sitting un-banked — say the word in the morning and I'll draft the paddock revision for your review.

Sources: [Entra + workforce federation guide](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-sign-in-microsoft-entra-id) · [Okta pricing](https://www.okta.com/pricing/) · [Auth0 plans](https://www.okta.com/pricing/auth0/)

message.content: I'm perfectly fine with the IdP selection being Manor time selected... I think.  We should probably discuss, now that I see me typing those words.  Hmm, ceremonies to switch out IdP sounds like a headache but not a nightmare.  Any more thoughts before I rest this for tonight?

content.text:
Manor-time is, I think, the architecturally honest answer — and conveniently, GCP's resource model already voted: workforce pools are **org-level** resources, so the pool-and-provider pair structurally lives at the Manor whether we like it or not. A few thoughts to rest on, then I'll stop:

1. **Manor-time selection aligns with a cinch you already made.** The paddock says "identity scope is a tier property: federates are IdP-scoped; the IdP is the census." Manor-scoped IdP selection is just that sentence becoming mechanism: the payor's founding gestures gain *establish-federation* (pool + provider + attribute mapping, once), and levy inscribes the resulting facts into each depot's RBRD. The depot never chooses an IdP; it records the manor's.

2. **It buys you something the paddock currently defers.** With one pool per manor, a person is the *same* `principal://` subject in every depot under it — the one-identity-across-depots property you assigned to "federation, where the IdP is the census" arrives free. Terrier entries reference stable subjects; enfranchising the same human into a second depot is a pure grant, no new identity.

3. **The switch ceremony is softer than headache-grade.** A pool holds **multiple providers concurrently**, so IdP migration is overlap-then-retire: payor adds provider B, governors re-enfranchise citizens (terrier-driven, mechanical — it's the idempotent-enfeoff heal), provider A retires when its last subject does. No flag day. The thing I'd cinch early is the negative: **one pool per manor, one live IdP as the norm** — per-depot IdP divergence is technically possible via extra pools and should be rejected until a real customer demands it, because it would quietly re-open the cross-depot identity story you just closed.

4. **The genuinely open engineering question for the spike** is the canon's own "concrete credential-source wiring" item: a run is many tabtarget *processes*, so the session token has to live somewhere between them — keychain or tmpfs scratch, ≤ session cap, never a regime. That's where the design meets the filesystem, and it's the part I'd want the spike to answer alongside the pool/STS legs.

Nothing tonight requires a decision. The full slate — premise shift, movement-3 recast, bridge/demolition policy, Entra-first + Keycloak-crucible, manor-time IdP selection with the one-pool cinch candidate, and the spike scope (now including session-cache wiring) — is all holding un-banked, and I'll draft the paddock revision for your review whenever you say so in the morning. Sleep well; this was a good evening's worth of shape.
